title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.1
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
keywords: not, secrets, credentials, sec.1, hardcode, long-lived, secret-like, literals, should, live, secret, management
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.2
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
keywords: shell, not, command, sec.2, build, commands, trusted, primitives, concatenated, strings, execution, paths
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.

title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.3
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
keywords: text, sql, query, should, sec.3, parameterize, instead, composing, dynamically, built, variables, difficult
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: sql_injection, dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.

title: Do not leave fallback secrets or test credentials in production code paths
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
summary: Backup credentials often become the least-audited credential in the system.
embedding_text:

language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id:

keywords: fallback, secrets, credentials, sec.4, not, leave, test, production, code, paths, backup, often
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.

title: Keep command execution APIs structured so the executable and arguments remain distinct
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
summary: Security review is much stronger when command construction never depends on ad hoc shell parsing.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.

title: Dynamic query structure should stay allow-listed even when values are parameterized
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.
summary: Parameter binding protects values, not uncontrolled structural SQL changes.

fix_guidance: Keep structural variation behind a small allow-list and continue binding values separately from the SQL text.
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.

title: Example, bootstrap, and fallback credentials in committed code should be treated as real secrets unless clearly impossible to activate
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.
summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.

summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.
keywords: bootstrap, credentials, secrets, sec.7, example, fallback, committed, code, should, treated, real, unless
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove committed fallback credentials or move them into an isolated local-only development path that cannot ship as a production default.

title: Avoid unsafe C string APIs on variable-length input
text: Functions such as strcpy, strcat, sprintf, and gets do not communicate destination capacity strongly enough for review and maintenance.
summary: Unbounded copy and formatting APIs are easy to misuse and hide buffer assumptions.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: unsafe_string_api
fix_guidance: Prefer size-aware interfaces and make destination capacity explicit at the call site.
text: Functions such as strcpy, strcat, sprintf, and gets do not communicate destination capacity strongly enough for review and maintenance.
fix_guidance: Prefer size-aware interfaces and make destination capacity explicit at the call site.

title: Manual cleanup jumps must keep all acquired resources in one structured path
text: In C, cleanup labels are sometimes necessary, but the pattern stays safe only when acquisition order and cleanup order remain obvious and single-sourced.
summary: goto-based cleanup can be acceptable, but only when it stays centralized and exhaustive.

category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: manual_cleanup_goto, malloc_free
fix_guidance: Keep cleanup labels centralized and ordered, and avoid multiple partially overlapping release branches.
text: In C, cleanup labels are sometimes necessary, but the pattern stays safe only when acquisition order and cleanup order remain obvious and single-sourced.
fix_guidance: Keep cleanup labels centralized and ordered, and avoid multiple partially overlapping release branches.

title: Size-sensitive copy and formatting calls should carry destination capacity in the same local context
text: Reviewers should not have to infer buffer size from distant declarations or comments. The call pattern should make capacity and truncation expectations visible where data crosses into the destination buffer.
summary: String and format operations are safer when the destination bound is obvious at the call site.

reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: unsafe_string_api, primitive_format_specifier
fix_guidance: Prefer bounded APIs and pass the destination size through the same local block that performs the write.
text: Reviewers should not have to infer buffer size from distant declarations or comments. The call pattern should make capacity and truncation expectations visible where data crosses into the destination buffer.
fix_guidance: Prefer bounded APIs and pass the destination size through the same local block that performs the write.

title: Cleanup labels should release resources in reverse acquisition order
text: A cleanup ladder is only maintainable when each label clearly corresponds to a prefix of acquired resources and teardown proceeds in reverse order without side paths.
summary: Structured goto cleanup stays understandable when ownership unwinds in one predictable direction.

category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: manual_cleanup_goto, malloc_free
fix_guidance: Keep one cleanup ladder and unwind resources in reverse acquisition order so later additions remain safe.
text: A cleanup ladder is only maintainable when each label clearly corresponds to a prefix of acquired resources and teardown proceeds in reverse order without side paths.
fix_guidance: Keep one cleanup ladder and unwind resources in reverse acquisition order so later additions remain safe.

title: Size-aware APIs still need an explicit truncation and null-termination contract
text: Even "safer" string and formatting functions can hide truncation, missing terminators, or silently altered output unless the surrounding code makes the expected outcome explicit.
summary: Choosing a bounded API is only the first step; callers and maintainers still need to know what happens at the boundary.

title: Surface ownership and capacity in function signatures
text: When a function consumes, fills, or frees caller-provided state, the signature and naming should make that responsibility explicit instead of relying on side knowledge.
summary: C interfaces should make caller responsibilities obvious through parameters and naming.

fix_guidance: Pass buffer size, ownership flags, or dedicated owner structs explicitly instead of encoding them implicitly in comments.
text: When a function consumes, fills, or frees caller-provided state, the signature and naming should make that responsibility explicit instead of relying on side knowledge.
fix_guidance: Pass buffer size, ownership flags, or dedicated owner structs explicitly instead of encoding them implicitly in comments.

title: API boundaries should expose writable buffer size and nullability expectations
text: Hidden ownership and capacity conventions turn ordinary integration work into guesswork. Project APIs are safer when callers can see the write contract in the signature instead of relying on tribal knowledge.
summary: Reviewers should be able to see whether a C function may write, own, or reject null directly from the boundary.

text: Project code is easier to review when formatting behavior is isolated behind narrow helpers instead of repeated ad hoc format strings with varying assumptions.
summary: Free-form format strings spread correctness risk across the codebase.

title: Release ownership from exactly one place
text: Explicit free paths must remain centralized and mechanically verifiable. When cleanup is spread through many branches, later maintenance easily introduces leaks or double-free bugs.
summary: Multiple independent free paths make lifetime reasoning fragile.

category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: malloc_free
fix_guidance: Use one owner or one cleanup path for each allocation family.
text: Explicit free paths must remain centralized and mechanically verifiable. When cleanup is spread through many branches, later maintenance easily introduces leaks or double-free bugs.
fix_guidance: Use one owner or one cleanup path for each allocation family.

title: Check allocation results before first use and before dependent work proceeds
text: In C, allocation failure is part of the contract. Review code so that newly allocated pointers are validated before dereference, initialization, or any follow-on work that assumes success.
summary: Manual allocation should fail closed at the point where ownership begins.

category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: malloc_free
fix_guidance: Check the returned pointer immediately and route failure through the same structured cleanup or error path used elsewhere in the function.
text: In C, allocation failure is part of the contract. Review code so that newly allocated pointers are validated before dereference, initialization, or any follow-on work that assumes success.
fix_guidance: Check the returned pointer immediately and route failure through the same structured cleanup or error path used elsewhere in the function.

title: Reallocation paths should preserve rollback semantics for the original owner
text: Manual memory resizing is easier to review when the code preserves the original owner until success is confirmed and the rollback path remains explicit.
summary: realloc-like ownership changes are risky when the old pointer can be lost on failure.
